entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Yeah, My name's Alex Cook. I'm Food Systems Development Manager at Carmanshire County Council. I helped establish the Food Network and subsequent Food Partnership. Been working for the council for three, nearly three years on food development and strategy for the Public Service Board. I support the Food Partnership by attending various working groups and steering groups. And the council supports the Food Partnership with elected member representation, so local politicians and specific job specific roles within the council around, particularly around grant funding, community grant funding. And we have a policy and partnerships team, so we have representation from them as well.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Fantastic. Can you give a brief overview of kind of the history of the Food Partnership and your involvement with this?

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: So I'm a chef and social entrepreneur before I worked at the council. So it was during 2020 or 2021, during COVID there was the local voluntary sector organization, CAVS association of Voluntary Services, decided to run some online network establishment events to develop a food network predominantly of local community food initiatives and projects providers, but also private sector restaurants, cafes. So the breadth of the food system, really. And yeah, after that network was established, I was consulting on a piece of work around procurement and we just thought that the Sustainable Food Places program was a natural fit. So, yeah, we had the network events and then when we could, we got people together for whole network events. We had some money allocated for a coordinator, so Augusta, before she was, she was a Food Networked development officer or something like that, some silly public sector title. So that kind of brought the Food Network together. But then we soon realized that we needed a kind of strategic steering group. So the steering group was born and then you almost had the Food Network and the Food Partnership kind of coexisting with the Food Partnership having more of that strategic level and the Food Network being more of your grassroots. So what we wanted it to do was operate with a good line of communication and support both ways, from grassroots into strategic, so public sector organisations. So that was the plan and at the same time connecting people in those organisations with each other so they could support each other and. Yeah, share resources, share knowledge, expertise.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great. Fantastic. What have been the main successes you see of the Food Partnership, particularly your relationship with it, like, what have you contributed? What has the council contributed?

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Wow. So the council manages and distributes its proportion of UK levelling up money, Shared Prosperity Fund. So we've had the Food Partnership developed a project called the Food Systems Development Project and that has drawn down about 1.2, 1.3 million investment into the food system locally. So that's been a big contribution. It's quite a new and innovative project with lots of different streams from public cows to community access, coordination and a significant amount of that money coming to the local farm here as a kind of hub where we were doing the supply chain and school menus and that kind of stuff. I think the biggest thing that the Council has contributed and the biggest success has been strategic buy in from senior management, from the chief exec, from the leader of the council, chair of the Public Service Board. So for them to understand the need to develop a strategy bringing the right people in and using the Food Partnership's expertise and knowledge of the local food system to develop that strategy and then supporting the kind of the policy alignment.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: I suppose really Augusta and I didn't really speak about this, but are you driving, is the council driving any grant applications for the Food Partnership or do you guys collaborate on that?

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: We collaborate on a couple of things. So most of the Food Partnerships money comes from Welsh Gov, through the WLGA to the Council and then back out to either a host organisation or directly funding elements of projects. So social farms and gardens and the Council, both members of the Food Partnership, they have a service level agreement. So the money comes in from Welsh, from WRGA and then goes straight out to support the coordination. Augusta's role we also then support the economic development team, which encompasses grants. So that's direct food aid provision, so capital and revenue streams to third sector organisations. So we support in the development of the specification. So we've identified the key weaknesses and intervention areas in the food system here. So we target those grants to where it's most needed in the local community, whether that's a move from food distribution. So kind of your standard food bank packages, emergency food aid packages to more pay as you feel, canteen models, growing models, green and blue infrastructure and then elements of revenue that can be directed to local producers rather than Aldi or Tesco or any other super bucket.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: What do you see as kind of the successes of that kind of funding model and are there any challenges? Like I know Augusta, her role has to be housed under social farms and garden because this is in its own entity.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Yeah, but that's the best place for it. The, the challenges of the Council's involvement are not, not being able to be as dynamic as other organizations, private or sector, that operate more of a business. They're quicker, they're more flexible, the outputs of those grants. From changing the grants in terms of we, we're moving towards a model that is more self sustaining rather than a model that you give a food bank money, it goes and spends the Money on fridges. It goes and fills the fridges with. Well, not even fridges. It might be store cupboards with non UK produced imports. Unhealthy understatement food. So I think by tailoring the grants that has brought more of this grow, cook meal share initiative together. But if I'm honest, the outputs, aside from the. What's actually measured so, you know, assets and food bank use, most of the outcomes are longer term than the grant funding. So I don't think we started to see the real benefits yet. Yeah, yeah.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: With that, what do you see next? Three to five years. What would success look like for this project?

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: So success in five years. We've got a food strategy with three key areas. I think success would, would mean hitting those targets, hitting five year targets. Specifics around percentages of organic or sustainably sustainability accredited food in the public sector across all of the Food Partnership partners, Health Board, nrw, everyone. So those key measurables would be success. I think the, the less tangible successes would be more strategic buy in from certain partners. Particularly we've had challenges because of some organizations being so broad and covering more than just the Carmarthenshire. So for example, the Health Board covers three counties and it's, it's just, it's a bit of a beast. What we want is more strategic and senior level buy in from them and more alignment with the rest of the Food Partnership partners. So some stronger policies, again delivering against the food strategy.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: So to be clear, because the Health Board kind of oversees three areas, kind of buy in is like less so. Because this is focused on Kamar this year.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Yes, definitely. But also they're just not very good at collaborating.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Just sort of any like particular things that you think are standing in the way or it's just the.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Yeah, I don't think they've had a recent change in management and structure which I'm hoping will lead to better, better collaboration or at least coordination. So hopefully with. Because they also. The Health Board also includes Public Health Wales. So we have great engagement with Public Health Wales under the Health Board. Community Dietetics we have really strong engagement with. But that's only because they've been there from the start. We have significant barriers around the operational management of hospital canteens, cafes, things like that, even patient food. So. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Is it a bandwidth issue or people.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Don'T see the bandwidth?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, bandwidth, yeah.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Bandwidth and priorities. Yeah, but they'll have their own task and finish. Not task working groups around food, but we can't get in and then those task and finish Groups will two of them have been and gone and with no outcomes, there's a, there's a, you don't have to keep this on file.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: But yeah, yeah, yeah.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: There's a systemic issue with the way that they function, which isn't outcomes focused. There's just too much going on and not enough autonomy to different elements. And where they have autonomy, they silo themselves.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Have they said kind of what needs to happen to get you into these working groups or it's just kind of stonewalling.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: I've met with the chief exec, I've met with the finance director and they both say they wish they could do more helpful. So the, again, this.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, this is between us. Yeah, yeah.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: So the chief exec has only been in position for, I don't know, six months a year. He says he can do less now than he could before.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Oh, wow. Okay.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: So it's like, well, I don't know, don't know what to do. Crazy.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Okay, last question. What advice would you give two other other councils looking to get involved in local partnerships or at the very least food systems change.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: If you don't, if you haven't got one already, develop a food strategy, get a working group together with support of your food partnership. Have a bare minimum, have some action plans, one page action plans and I think to food partnerships wanting to engage with local authorities. Go into your elected members because sometimes you get more things done in some ways by getting buy in from them. If you have a local cabinet member that has an interest or a portfolio with climate change or with communities and health or even rural estate, for example, if you can get them to a steering group or a working group, then you'll start to get some buy in and they'll start to point you in the right directions with people. But yeah, the council's not always. It's better than the health board. There are a lot of people in the council that have full time jobs around food and they need to be actively engaged with the food partnership.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, great. Fantastic. Okay.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Okay, great. So could you tell me a little bit about the Food Partnership and the background of it?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So Boyd Cigar Food is the local food partnership for Carmarthenshire in West Wales. We've been a Sustainable Food Places member for about two and a half years and we've been functioning for about four years and began in the height of the COVID pandemic.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great. What is the objective of the Food Partnership? How does it contribute to food systems change?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So Boyd Cigar Food is aiming to, through a collaborative process with communities and other stakeholders across the county at public service level and private sector and community sector level, as well as citizens to co create a food system which is diverse, relocalised, inclusive and sustainable and resilient.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Can you describe the main projects you have going?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So we have a variety of projects at the moment. The predominating project is the Food System Development project for Carmarthenshire. Again that was developed by listening to people through discussion groups from various backgrounds over the course of a year to really try and ensure that we're taking a place based response that's appropriate to our communities and our county. We're a large rural county. So the food System development project has three strands. One is around communications and building a good food movement. So looking at how we can bring particularly our disadvantaged communities along on a journey around celebrating the growing cooking and meal sharing of foods and strengthening food provision in their community to ensure that everybody has access to healthy and sustainable food. So that's a communication strategy and then we have strategic land for public goods which is really the farm project here in Llanathany. So looking at using a county council owned farm to look at how public lands can contribute to strategic objectives from increasing locally sourced ingredients for public menus, sequestering carbon, nature recovery, health well being and education activities based at the farm and back out in communities. So we have school groups coming, local government representatives, you know, cross partnership meetings, cross organizational meetings, as well as community groups coming and doing activities around growing cooking, eating and nutritional awareness building. Okay, so the third strand, I should just cut that bit because I can't remember what the third strand is. Not very helpful. Communications. Strategic land for public goods. Enabling inclusive access. So the third strand of the food system development project is about enabling inclusive access. So that's really focusing on some of the clusters of wards in the county that experience the highest levels of deprivation on the Wales index of multiple deprivation and really trying to empower them to find ways of, you know, growing more in their communities, meal sharing and cooking together to really build an inclusive community food level. Sorry. So the third strand is our enabling inclusive access work, which is led by Public Health Wales partners and our community health improvement dietitians, as well as representatives from voluntary sector and community growing sector to really strengthen within communities the local solutions from accessing healthy and sustainable seasonal fruit and vegetables.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great. Can you describe within the Food Partnership who are kind of the main support staff here on the farm, your other coordinators and then we'll talk about kind of external support like the health board, etc.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So, through the food system development project, we've been able to. Which is shared prosperity, we've been able to support a project manager based at the council as well as project support administration. We have two full time growing staff here on the farm growing the vegetables for our public procurement pilots, as well as exploring routes to market for private and voluntary sector provision. As well, we have some casual staff here at the farm that support the growing element too seasonally. And then we employ within the Health board, a community health improvement dietitian, which is more focused on our work out in satellite community areas to enable that greater provision and access to particularly fruit and vegetables.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great. That's Alana, right? Yeah. I'm hoping to speak to her this week. We were supposed to call last week but she was feeling sick.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: I'm just. Oh, I didn't mention. We also have funded, actually through Welsh governments, my own role, as well as two other roles within social farms and gardens that are around community food access and the other is around activities and events and administrative support.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yes. Speaking of which, can you introduce yourself in your role? Yes.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So my name is Augusta Lewis. I coordinate Boyd's Cigar Food, the local food partnership for Carmarthenshire, and I'm based at social farms and gardens.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great. And can you describe kind of any other actors that you collaborate with on a regular basis?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So we collaborate dynamically with a really broad range of partners, from third sector umbrella organisations to third sector organisations themselves, be they directly involved with community food provision. Or on a tangent, but they have elements of enabling access to local and sustainable food, as well as private sector incubation organisations that support organisations that support businesses to be able to develop their products, food products to go to market Castellhau, which is the logistics and distribution provider for most public sector recipients across the county, as well as a lot of hospitality and retail. And then we work with all the public service board members that ranges from the council and various departments, Health board, Natural Resources Wales, the environmental regulator and others that work within the public sector as well.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great. What are the main obstacles the organisation has faced and how have you managed them?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So the main obstacles that the partnership has faced really are fairly minimal. We've been very lucky to have strong strategic support, mainly from the Wellbeing of Future Generations act and the way that public service boards operate, but also because food is a cross cutting theme and so can deliver against multiple strategic objectives, from nature recovery net zero to health well being and community cohesion as well as climate. Sorry, because that's net zero. So in terms of obstacles, the main obstacles that we've faced has been funding inconsistency of funding. So we're very fortunate in Wales, comparative to the other four nations of the United Kingdom, to have Welsh government support from the Ministry of Social justice, particularly in recognition of the work we do to break down barriers to healthy and sustainable food for all, and increasingly from other aspects of Welsh government, particularly around the incoming sustainable farming scheme and supporting diversification. So that's been amazing, but it is, you know, from one tax year to the next kind of. We're never sure what's coming next. So that inconsistency of funding has kind of. It stops the kind of longer term planning sometimes.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, great. Just two more questions beyond what you just mentioned, any other successes that you want kind of featured? And that could be like project objectives or even like more, you know, how is the community building going? How's the general support for the initiative?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: Yeah, I think, I mean, I think the most unique aspect is the farm, but, you know, I don't know whether I should say something about that again or. You can get that.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: I can get that from here. So maybe talk about the community, the workshops, how are those going?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: Yes. So here at the Farm ans out in communities, we're taking the approach of listening to community representatives and people with lived experiences to really try and be responsive to what they're wanting and needing in our project of design and development. So one example is here at the Farm. We had a group of community representatives here last week at the farm to really kind of drill down into how could the site be used to support more activities for the community and as well in communities across the county, led by our health board colleagues, to really ask communities what assets there are already, what projects, what skills, what are the key stakeholders already there to bring together a kind of co developed approach that's unique to that particular community. So very, very bespoke to each community we work in.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: What advice would you give to someone else hoping to contribute to a local food partnership or broader food Systems change?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: Well, I think there's lots. So, you know, most local authority areas across the United Kingdom now have a local food partnership, thanks to the work of Sustain and the Sustainable Food Places Framework, which really gives quite clear guidance on how you can develop a holistic systems approach in your area. So whether you're a primary food producer, a community volunteer or a member of the local council, whether that's county level or community level, you can get involved to make a difference through your local food partnership.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great, that's a good sound bite. And then just quickly, can you talk about the Sustainable Food Places Framework and kind of how you guys sit under that? Because I think that's interesting, although I.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: Don'T sometimes I get to, like, number five of the key areas.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Don't worry about the key areas, just kind of say that you're one of the, you know, 28 partnerships. Just. So we have that in there.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So in Wales, we are one of the 22. No, we're not, actually. We're really not. Because actually, not everybody's a member of Sustainable Features Places yet. They haven't got across the line.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: There is a number about which one there is maybe just say, like, we're.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: One of them, seventh or the ninth to become members, but I don't know what we're up to now. So more and more local food partnerships in Wales, where we've had Welsh government support, are joining the Sustainable Food Places Scheme because it offers a really fantastic and reliable framework for holistic development across six key areas. So we are members, along with increasing. Yeah, that's it.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Perfect. Great.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Piers
entries.text: Introduce yourself and your role at the farm.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Okay. My name is Piers Lunt, I'm hedge grower on Brimenda Surfer County Farm, working on food system development projects and the future generations menu project.

entries.speaker: Piers
entries.text: How does the farm fit into like the larger Food Local Food Partnership?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: The project that started here on the farm was the brainchild of the Local Food Partnership. And so, yeah, it's quite a kind of keystone part of the whole kind of food system that's in development in Carmarthenshire. We are growing mostly for schools, but we're also trying to grow other public sector markets as well, essentially to get nutritious, organic, local and seasonal food on the public plate. So yeah, as a part of the way that the Food Partnership does is quite key. Also ties in with some of the community objectives of the partnership as well.

entries.speaker: Piers
entries.text: Great. Can you describe the farm, its size, kind of how it's broken down and maybe some of the other facilities on site?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Sure. The farm is about 103 or 104 acres, depending on who you speak to. It's ex intensive dairy, so it's been cut basically for silage for a number of decades. But historically before that it was much more mixed as farms generally were. So yeah, so we've set up a intensive market garden on a two acre field which has been enclosed with animal fencing. On top of that we're developing a community garden. We've also got lots of infrastructure. You know, there's a whole range of outbuildings which are ripe for redevelopment. There's a six bedroom farmhouse as well, which is in need of quite a significant bit of investment to kind of get that back into, you know, usable kind of condition again. Really? Yeah, the Food Partnership has some kind of big ambitions potentially for the site. As long as we can get a tenancy, that's the really kind of key thing. So at the moment the farm is between tenancy. The previous tenant left just over two years ago. He's been here for 50 years, right from when the council first kind of bought the site and tenanted it. At the moment it can't actually be re tenanted because of the amount of investment that's needed. But also the tower path that's being built on the land. The council have felt that, you know, that kind of key work needs to be done before they could tenant it again. So the Food Partnership would like to constitute as a group in some way so that we can actually tender for the tenancy when it comes up. And we're expecting that could be within the next kind of three to four years and then we have lots of different ideas floating about as what the site could be. Some of those ideas are already being put in train. So the community garden is a really key part of that that's really accessible. It's just off the yard. We've built a roundhouse there which is perfect for hosting all manner of groups large enough to accommodate 30 pupil class size. So we can get, you know, primary schools, even secondary schools if we can actually get them here. But various other community groups can get involved in that as well. We put a polytunnel up in there and we're sort of working on co design with the community. So we've got groups coming on periodically. They're actually talking to us about what they might like to see in a community garden. And so that's a very kind of iterative process that's kind of ongoing at the moment in terms of the other facilities that we've got here. We have a pack house obviously, which is something that's really key for veg growing and we'd like to develop that eventually into what's called a food hub. So we can aggregate produce here from other growers. If we can grow that public sector market as well, then we can offer that market to those other growers. They can come here with their produce. They can actually use the packing facilities themselves if they want to. We need to increase our storage facilities. That's another thing that we're looking at at the moment, particularly climate controlled storage. And then we could aggregate the produce here. It can go out through one of the distributors locally and onto the public plate. Beyond that, you know, we've got this barn here which would be, I mean, at the moment it's a kind of event space. So we host groups in here quite regularly. It would be nice to develop that. There's also potentially opportunity for developing a cafe, an on site shop. We might even be able to bring third party businesses on here that kind of fit with the ethos of what we're doing, you know, and potentially kind of tenant discrete parts of the infrastructure that way. So yeah, lots of exciting stuff going on in terms of the growing. As I mentioned, you know, we've got the intensive market garden, which as the name implies, you know, is very intensive. It's all organic. Everything we do here is organic and we're imminently going into organic conversion in fact. But yeah, we're using kind of small scale hand tools, two wheel tractors, that kind of stuff out in that garden. But we are looking to scale up our Veg production into field scale, which will obviously be four wheel tractor with all the kind of attachments and stuff that go with that. And that really is just to increase our capacity in terms of what we can supply into primarily the public sector.

entries.speaker: Piers
entries.text: Yeah, Augusta was telling me about the equipment kind of not renting but taking the equipment from local farmers. Can you kind of speak to that aim of like not only this being a place for community to come learn about growing and get produce, but also as a learning hub.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: So social farms and gardens were able to facilitate a Welsh government grant a couple of years ago specifically for the development of called a machinery ring. So basically, you know, they got together with a load of local growers and said, well what kind of kit do you need? You know, they went through that kind of co design process and off the back of that bought loads of really, really great kit for small scale market gardening. So we're hosting that here on the farm because it's quite a central point in the county. The original idea was that that would be specifically for Carmarthenshire growers, but we've had so much interest from Keredig in Pembrokeshire, across the borders as well. But we decided to kind of extend it out to anybody who's willing to travel here and pick up the kit basically. So it works with an online booking system. People can go online onto a website called MyTurn, the Carmarthenshire Library of Things, and they can book stuff out for a very nominal fee. So originally it was free, but now we are charging a nominal fee basically because obviously there's maintenance and administration that comes with hosting a ring. So that is kind of paying for those kinds of elements really. So yeah, it started with small scale, small market garden kit, a number of two wheel tractors and various attachments that go with that, flail mowers, ploughs, power harrows, that kind of stuff, as well as various other tools that actually were predominantly focused on no dig market gardening. There's quite a few market gardeners around using that particular technique, but it's also expanded now. There was a second round of funding that was used to buy tractor attachments for field scale growing as well. So we've started accumulating some of that kit on the farm here as well. Obviously the nice thing about us hosting it here is we can kind of use it quite regularly, but also we're kind of learning how to maintain the stuff, how to look after it and obviously how to use some of it. You know, some of that stuff has been a bit unfamiliar to even Us here as growers. So that's been really great and just a great way of networking and meeting other growers around the place who are doing similar things.

entries.speaker: Piers
entries.text: Can you have a little sound bite about what has been the major successes in your view of the farm?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Okay. The major success in the first year was just the amount of food that we were able to grow on a really small space in a really contracted season. The project was late getting started, you know, we were still waiting for bits of kit to arrive, the weather was awful, so we didn't really get cultivating until May, even sort of into June. So between then and the new year, we produced six and a half tonnes of food from about a third of an acre. So, you know, we were quite pleased with that. That was a major success. I think also we've been really successful at using the capital aspect of our grant funding to put in all the infrastructure that we need. So, you know, from a market gardener's point of view, you know, it's been great. It's all singing, all dancing. We've got all the polytunnels, we've got the irrigation, you know, we've got all, all the tools that we need. We've been able to buy all the seeds and seed trays and all that kind of stuff. So I think we've been really successful at using our resources quite carefully and to the best advantage. But I would say the main success, the thing that really gets me fired up is the fact that food is going into schools, it's going into local care homes, we've been selling to local businesses. We're also providing into the third sector as well. So Fair Share Cymru had quite a lot of produce from us last summer. Local food banks, community kitchens and that, all of that has just been an absolutely brilliant networking experience. So we've really got to know a lot of people around the county who are involved in developing the food system in all these kind of discreet areas. And it's just been great to kind of link up all of these different cool things that are kind of going on around place and bring it all together under the kind of umbrella of the Local Food Partnership. So that's been really successful and really exciting.

entries.speaker: Piers
entries.text: Amazing. Last question. What advice would you give to someone else trying to design kind of a farm that's more socially motivated and more sustainable?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: The main thing is really go out and speak to your community if you want to develop something for the community on a farm like this, you can't do it top down, you can't come in and say we're going to design something like this and then bring the community in. You have to bring the community in right at the beginning and work with them, talk to them about what they want and what they need and then bring that into the design process. That doesn't mean that there shouldn't be oversight because there's going to be lots and lots of different ideas to kind of pull in together. So it does need an individual or a committee that are going to kind of bring all of those different elements together in the most successful way. But there's nothing better than actually talking to the community about what they want. And then beyond that, really it's remembering that community isn't just about people, it's also about the natural environment as well. So if you want to have genuine community benefit, then it has to have ecological benefit as well. So you need to think about those things in the round. Really great.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Yeah, My name's Alex Cook. I'm Food Systems Development Manager at Carmanshire County Council. I helped establish the Food Network and subsequent Food Partnership. Been working for the council for three, nearly three years on food development and strategy for the Public Service Board. I support the Food Partnership by attending various working groups and steering groups. And the council supports the Food Partnership with elected member representation, so local politicians and specific job specific roles within the council around, particularly around grant funding, community grant funding. And we have a policy and partnerships team, so we have representation from them as well.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Fantastic. Can you give a brief overview of kind of the history of the Food Partnership and your involvement with it?

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: So I'm a chef and social entrepreneur before I worked at the council. So it was during 2020 or 2021, during COVID there was the local voluntary sector organization, CAVS association of Voluntary Services, decided to run some online network establishment events to develop a food network predominantly of local community food initiatives and projects providers, but also private sector restaurants, cafes. So the breadth of the food system, really. And yeah, after that network was established, I was consulting on a piece of work around procurement and we just thought that the Sustainable Food Places program was a natural fit. So yeah, we had the network events and then when we could, we got people together for whole network events. We had some money allocated for a coordinator, so Augusta, before she was, she was a Food Networked development officer or something like that, some silly public sector title. So that kind of brought the Food Network together. But then we soon realized that we needed a kind of strategic steering group. So the steering group was born and then you almost had the Food Network and the Food Partnership kind of coexisting with the Food Partnership having more of that strategic level and the Food Network being more of your grassroots. So what we wanted it to do was operate with a good line of communication and support both ways, from grassroots into strategic, so public sector organizations. So that was the plan and at the same time connecting people in those organisations with each other so they could support each other and. Yeah, share resources, share knowledge, expertise.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great. Fantastic. What have been the main successes you see of the Food Partnership, particularly your relationship with it? Like what have you contributed? What has the council contributed?

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Wow. So the council manages and distributes its proportion of UK leveling up money, Shared Prosperity Fund. So we've had the Food Partnership developed a project called the Food Systems Development Project and that has drawn down about 1.2, 1.3 million investment into the food system locally. So that's been a big contribution. It's quite a new and innovative project with lots of different streams from public cows to community access, coordination and a significant amount of that money coming to the local farm here as a kind of hub where we were doing the supply chain and school menus and that kind of stuff. I think the biggest thing that the Council has contributed and the biggest success has been strategic buy in from senior management, from the chief exec, from the leader of the council, chair of the Public Service Board. So for them to understand the need to develop a strategy bringing the right people in and using the Food Partnership's expertise and knowledge of the local food system to develop that strategy and then supporting the kind of the policy alignment.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: I suppose really Augusta and I didn't really speak about this, but are you driving, is the council driving any grant applications for the Food Partnership or do you guys collaborate on that?

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: We collaborate on a couple of things. So most of the Food Partnerships money comes from Welsh Gov, through the WLGA to the Council and then back out to either a host organisation or directly funding elements of projects. So social farms and gardens and the Council, both members of the Food Partnership, they have a service level agreement. So the money comes in from Welsh, from WRGA and then goes straight out to support the coordination. Augusta's role we also then support the economic development team, which encompasses grants. So that's direct food aid provision, so capital and revenue streams to third sector organisations. So we support in the development of the specification. So we've identified the key weaknesses and intervention areas in the food system here. So we target those grants to where it's most needed in the local community, whether that's a move from food distribution. So kind of your standard food bank packages, emergency food aid packages to more pay as you feel, canteen models, growing models, green and blue infrastructure, and then elements of revenue that can be directed to local producers rather than Aldi or Tesco or any other super bucket.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: What do you see as kind of the successes of that kind of funding model? And are there any challenges? Like I know Augusta, you know her role has to be housed under social farms and garden because this is in its own entity.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Yeah, but that's the best place for it. The, the challenges of the Council's involvement are not, not being able to be as dynamic as other organizations, private or sector, that operate more of a business. They're quicker, they're more flexible, the outputs of those grants. From changing the grants in terms of we, we're moving towards a model that is more self sustaining rather than a model that you give a food bank money, it goes and Spends the money on fridges, it goes and fills the fridges with. Well, not even fridges. It might be store cupboards with non UK produced imports, unhealthy understated food. So I think by tailoring the grants that has brought more of this grow, cook meal share initiative together. But if I'm honest, the outputs, aside from the. What's actually measured so, you know, assets and food bank use, most of the, the outcomes are longer term than the grant funding. So I don't think, think we've started to see the real benefits yet with that.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: What do you see next three to five years. What would success look like for this project?

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: So success in five years. We've got a food strategy with three key areas. I think success would mean hitting those targets, hitting five year targets, specifics around percentages of organic or sustainably sustainability accredited food in the public sector across all of the Food Partnership partners, Health Board, nrw, everyone. So those key measurables would be success. I think the, the less tangible successes would be more strategic buy in from certain partners. Particularly we've had challenges because of some organizations being so broad and covering more than just the Carmarthenshire. So for example, the Health Board covers three counties and it's, it's just, it's a bit of a beast. What we want is more strategic and senior level buy in from them and more alignment with the rest of the Food Partnership partners. So some stronger policies. Again, delivering against the food strategy.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: So to be clear, because the Health Board kind of oversees three areas, kind of. Mine is like less so because this is focused on Kamartha Shere.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Yes, definitely. But also they're just not very good at collaborating.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Just sort of any like particular things that you think are standing in the way or it's just the.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Yeah, I don't think they've had a recent change in management and structure which I'm hoping will lead to better, better collaboration or at least coordination. So hopefully with. Because they also. The Health Board also includes Public Health Wales. So we have great engagement with Public Health Wales under the Health Board. Community Dietetics we have really strong engagement with. But that's only because they've been there from the start. We have significant barriers around the operational management of hospital canteens, cafes, things like that, even patient food. So. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Is it a bandwidth issue or people.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Don'T see the bandwidth?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, Bandwidth, yeah.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Bandwidth and priorities.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: But they'll have their own task and finish. Not task working groups around food, but we can't get in. And then those task and finish groups will two of them have been and gone and with no outcomes. There's a, there's a, you don't have to keep this on file.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: But yeah, yeah, yeah.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: There's a systemic issue with the way that they function which isn't outcomes focused. There's just too much going on and not enough autonomy to different elements. And where they have autonomy, they silo themselves.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Have they said kind of what needs to happen to get you into these working groups or it's just kind of stonewalling.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: I've met with the chief exec, I've met with the finance director and they both say they wish they could do more helpful. So the, again, this.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, this is between us. Yeah, yeah.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: So the chief exec has only been in position for, I don't know, six months a year. He says he can do less now than he could before.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Oh, wow. Okay.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: So it's like, well, I don't know, don't know what to do. Crazy.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Okay, last question. What advice would you give two other other councils looking to get involved in local partnerships or at the very least food systems change.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: If you don't, if you haven't got one already, develop a food strategy, get a working group together with support of your food partnership. Have a bare minimum, have some action plans, one page action plans and I think to food partnerships wanting to engage with local authorities. Go into your elected members because sometimes you get more things done in some ways by getting buy in from them. If you have a local cabinet member that has an interest or a portfolio with climate change or with communities and health or even rural estate, for example, if you can get them to a steering group or a working group, then you'll start to get some buy in and they'll start to point you in the right directions with people. But yeah, the council's not always. It's better than the health board. There are a lot of people in the council that have full time jobs around food and they need to be actively engaged with the food partnership.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, yeah, great, fantastic.

entries.speaker: Alex
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Okay, great. So could you tell me a little bit about the Food Partnership and the background of it?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So Boyd Cigar Food is the local food partnership for Carmarthenshire in West Wales. We've been a Sustainable Food Places member for about two and a half years and we've been functioning for about four years and began in the height of the COVID pandemic.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great. What is the objective of the Food Partnership? How does it contribute to food systems change?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So Boyd Cigar Food is aiming to, through a collaborative process with communities and other stakeholders across the county at public service level and private sector and community sector level, as well as citizens to co create a food system which is diverse, relocalised, inclusive and sustainable and resilient.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Can you describe the main projects you have going?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So we have a variety of projects at the moment. The predominating project is the Food System Development project for Carmarthenshire. Again that was developed by listening to people through discussion groups from various backgrounds over the course of a year to really try and ensure that we're taking a place based response that's appropriate to our communities and our county. We're a large rural county. So the food System development project has three strands. One is around communications and building a good food movement. So looking at how we can bring particularly our disadvantaged communities along on a journey around celebrating the growing cooking and meal sharing of foods and strengthening food provision in their community to ensure that everybody has access to healthy and sustainable food. So that's a communication strategy and then we have strategic land for public goods which is really the farm project here in Llanathany. So looking at using a county council owned farm to look at how public lands can contribute to strategic objectives from increasing locally sourced ingredients for public menus, sequestering carbon, nature recovery, health well being and education activities based at the farm and back out in communities. So we have school groups coming, local government representatives, you know, cross partnership meetings, cross organizational meetings, as well as community groups coming and doing activities around growing cooking, eating and nutritional awareness building. Okay, so the third strand, I should just cut that bit because I can't remember what third strand. It's not very helpful.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Communications.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: Strategic land for public goods enabling inclusive access. So the third strand of the food system development project is about enabling inclusive access. So that's really focusing on some of the clusters of wards in the county that experience the highest levels of deprivation on the Wales Index of Multiple deprivation and really trying to empower them to find ways of, you know, growing more in their communities, meal sharing and cooking together to really Build an inclusive community food level. Sorry. So the third strand is our enabling inclusive access work, which is led by Public Health Wales partners and our community health improvement dietitians, as well as representatives from voluntary sector and community growing sector to really strengthen within communities the local solutions from accessing healthy and sustainable seasonal fruit and vegetables.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great. Can you describe, within the Food Partnership who are kind of the main support staff here on the farm, your other coordinators and then we'll talk about kind of external support like the health board, etc.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So through the food system development project, we've been able to. Which is shared prosperity, we've been able to support a project manager based at the council as well as project support administration. We have two full time growing staff here on the farm growing the vegetables for our public procurement pilots, as well as exploring routes to market for private and voluntary sector provision. As well, we have some casual staff here at the farm that support the growing elements too seasonally. And then we employ within the Health board, a community health improvement dietitian, which is more focused on our work out in satellite community areas to enable that greater provision and access to particularly fruit and vegetables.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great. That's Alana, right? Yeah. I'm hoping to speak to her this week. We were supposed to call last week but she was feeling sick.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: I'm just. Oh, I didn't mention. We also have funded, actually through Welsh governments, my own role, as well as two other roles within social farms and gardens that are around community food access and the other is around activities and events and administrative support.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yes. Speaking of which, can you introduce yourself in your role? Yes.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So my name is Augusta Lewis. I coordinate Boyd Cigar Food, the local food partnership for Carmarthenshire, and I'm based at social farms and gardens.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great. And can you describe kind of any other actors that you collaborate with on a regular basis?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So we collaborate dynamically with a really broad range of partners, from third sector umbrella organisations to third sector organisations themselves, be they directly involved with community food provision. Or on a tangent, but they have elements of enabling access to local and sustainable food, as well as private sector incubation organisations that support organisations that support businesses to be able to develop their products, food products to go to market Castellhau, which is the logistics and distribution provider for most public sector recipients across the county, as well as a lot of hospitality and retail. And then we work with all the public service board members that ranges from the council and various departments, Health Board, Natural Resources Wales, the environmental regulator and others that work within the public sector as well.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great. What are the main obstacles the organisation has faced and how have you managed them?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So the main obstacles that the partnership has faced really are fairly minimal. We've been very lucky to have strong strategic support, mainly from the Wellbeing of Future Generations act and the way that public service boards operate, but also because food is a cross cutting theme and so can deliver against multiple strategic objectives, from nature recovery net zero, to health wellbeing and community cohesion as well as climate. Sorry, because that's net zero. So in terms of obstacles, the main obstacles that we've faced has been funding inconsistency of funding. So we're very fortunate in Wales, comparative to the other four nations of the United Kingdom, to have Welsh government support from the Ministry of Social justice, particularly in recognition of the work we do to break down barriers to healthy and sustainable food for all, and increasingly from other aspects of Welsh government, particularly around the incoming sustainable farming scheme and supporting diversification. So that's been amazing, but it is, you know, from one tax year to the next, kind of. We're never sure what's coming next. So that inconsistency of funding has kind of. It stops the kind of longer term planning sometimes.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, great. Just two more questions, beyond what you just mentioned, any other successes that you want kind of featured? And that could be like project objectives or even like more, you know, how is the community building going? How's the general support for the initiative?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: Yeah, I think, I mean, I think the most unique aspect is the farm, but, you know, I don't know whether I should say something about that again or. You can get that.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: I can get that from Pierce. So maybe talk about the community, the workshops, how are those going?

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: Yes. So here at the Farm and out in communities, we're taking the approach of listening to community representatives and people with lived experiences to really try and be responsive to what they're wanting and needing in our project of design and development. So one example is here at the Farm. We had a group of community representatives here last week at the Farm to really kind of drill down into how could the site be used to support more activities for the community and as well in communities across the county, led by our health Board colleagues, to really ask communities what assets there are already, what projects, what skills, what are the key stakeholders already there, to bring together a kind of co developed approach that's unique to that particular community. So very, very bespoke to each community we work in.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Last question, what advice would you give to someone else hoping to contribute to a local food partnership or broader Food systems change.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: Well, I think there's lots. Okay, stop that again. So, you know, most local authority areas across the United Kingdom now have a local food partnership, thanks to the work of Sustain and the Sustainable Food Places Framework, which really gives quite clear guidance on how you can develop a holistic systems approach in your area. So whether you're a primary food producer, a community volunteer or. Or a member of the local council, whether that's county level or community level, you can get involved to make a difference through your local food partnership.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Great, that's a good sound bite. And then just quickly, can you talk about the Sustainable Food Places Framework and kind of how you guys sit under that? Because I think that's interesting.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: Although I don't. Sometimes I get to, like, number five of the key areas.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Don't, don't worry about the key areas. Just kind of say that you're one of the, you know, 28 partnerships. Just so we have that in there.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: So in Wales, we are one of the 22. No, we're not, actually. We're really not. Because actually, not everybody's member of Sustainable Food Places yet. They haven't got across the line.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: There is a number about which one. There is maybe just say, like, we're one of them.

entries.speaker: Augustain
entries.text: Yeah. The seventh or the ninth to become members, but I don't know what we're up to now. So more and more local food partnerships in Wales, where we've had Welsh government support, are joining the Sustainable Food Places Scheme because it offers a really fantastic and reliable framework for holistic development across six key areas. So we are members, along with increasing. Yeah, that's it.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Just perfect. Great.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Piers
entries.text: Introduce yourself and your role at the farm.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Okay. My name is Piers Lunt. I'm hedge grower on Brimenda Surfer County Farm, working on food system development projects and the future generations menu project.

entries.speaker: Piers
entries.text: How does the farm fit into like the larger Food Local Food Partnership?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: The project that started here on the farm was the brainchild of the Local Food Partnership. And so, yeah, it's quite a kind of keystone part of the whole kind of food system that's in development in Carmarthenshire. We are growing mostly for schools, but we're also trying to grow other public sector markets as well, essentially to get nutritious, organic, local and seasonal food on the public plate. So yeah, as a part of the way that the Food Partnership does, it's is quite key. Also ties in with some of the community objectives of the partnership as well.

entries.speaker: Piers
entries.text: Great. Can you describe the farm, its size, kind of how it's broken down and maybe some of the other facilities on site?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Sure. The farm is about 103 or 104 acres, depending on who you speak to. It's ex intensive dairy, so it's been cut basically for silage for a number of decades. But historically before that it was much more mixed as farms, you know, generally were. So yeah, so we've set up a intensive market garden on a two acre field which has been enclosed with animal fencing. On top of that we're developing a community garden. We've also got lots of infrastructure. You know, there's a whole range of outbuildings which are ripe for redevelopment. There's a six bedroom farmhouse as well, which is in need of quite a significant bit of investment to kind of get that back into, you know, usable kind of condition again. Really? Yeah, The Food Partnership has some kind of big ambitions potentially for the site. As long as we can get a tenancy, that's the really kind of key thing. So at the moment the farm is between tenancy. The previous tenant left just over two years ago. He's been here for 50 years, right from when the council first kind of bought the site and tenanted it. At the moment it can't actually be re tenanted because of the amount of investment that's needed. But also the tower path that's being built on the land. The council have felt that, you know, that kind of key work needs to be done before they could tenant it again. So the Food Partnership would like to constitute as a group in some way so that we can actually tender for the tenancy when it comes up. And we're expecting that could be within the next kind of three to four years. And then we have lots of different ideas floating about as what the site could be. Some of those ideas are already being put in train. So the community garden is a really key part of that that's really accessible. It's just off the yard. We've built a roundhouse there which is perfect for hosting all manner of groups large enough to accommodate 30 pupil class size. So we can get, you know, primary schools, even secondary schools if we can actually get them here. But various other community groups can get involved in that as well. We put a polytunnel up in there and we're sort of working on co design with the community. So we've got groups coming on periodically. They're actually talking to us about what they might like to see in a community garden. And so that's a very kind of iterative process that's kind of ongoing at the moment in terms of the other facilities that we've got here. We have a pack house obviously, which is something that's really key for veg growing and we'd like to develop that eventually into what's called a food hub. So we can aggregate produce here from other growers. If we can grow that public sector market as well, then we can offer that market to those other growers. They can come here with their produce. They can actually use the packing facilities themselves if they want to. We need to increase our storage facilities. That's another thing that we're looking at at the moment, particularly climate controlled storage. And then we could aggregate the produce here. It can go out through one of the distributors locally and onto the public plate. Beyond that, you know, we've got this barn here which would be, I mean, at the moment it's a kind of events. So we host groups in here quite regularly. It would be nice to develop that. There's also potentially opportunity for developing a cafe, an on site shop. We might even be able to bring third party businesses on here that kind of fit with the ethos of what we're doing and potentially kind of tenant discrete parts of the infrastructure that way. So yeah, lots of exciting stuff going on in terms of the growing. As I mentioned, we've got the intensive market going garden, which as the name implies, you know, is very intensive. It's all organic. Everything we do here is organic and we're imminently going into organic conversion in fact. But yeah, we're using kind of small scale hand tools, two wheel tractors, that kind of stuff out in that garden. But we are looking to scale up Our veg production into field scale, which will obviously be four wheel tractors with all the kind of attachments and stuff that go with that. And that really is just to increase our capacity in terms of what we can supply into primarily the public sector.

entries.speaker: Piers
entries.text: Yeah, Augusta was telling me about the equipment kind of not renting but taking the equipment from local farmers. Can you kind of speak to that aim of like not only this being a place for community to come learn about growing and get produce, but also as a learning hub.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: So social farms and Gardens were able to facilitate a Welsh government grant a couple of years ago specifically for the development of what's called a machinery ring. So basically, you know, they got together with a load of local growers and said, well, what kind of kit do you need? You know, they went through that kind of co design process and off the back of that bought loads of really, really great kit for small scale market gardening. So we're hosting that here on the farm because it's quite a central point in the county. The original idea was that that would be specifically for Carmarthenshire growers, but we've had so much interest from Cerridiggin and Pembrokeshire across the borders as well. But we decided to kind of extend it out to anybody who's willing to travel here and pick up the kit, basically. So it works with an online booking system. People can go online onto a website called MyTurn, the Carmarthenshire Library of Things, and they can book stuff out for a very nominal fee. So originally it was free, but now we are charging a nominal fee basically because obviously there's maintenance and administration that comes with hosting a ring. So that is kind of paying for those kinds of elements really. So yeah, it started with small scale, small market garden kit, a number of two wheeled tractors and various attachments that go with that, flail mowers, ploughs, power harrows, that kind of stuff, as well as various other tools that actually were predominantly focused on no dig market gardening. There's quite a few market gardeners around using that particular technique, but it's also expanded now. There was a second round of funding that was used to buy tractor attachments for field scale growing as well. So we've started accumulating some of that kit on the farm here as well. Obviously the nice thing about us hosting it here is that we can kind of use it quite regularly, but also we're kind of learning how to maintain the stuff, how to look after it and obviously how to use some of it. Some of that stuff has been A bit unfamiliar to even us here as growers. So. So that's been really great and just a great way of networking and meeting other growers around the place who are doing similar things.

entries.speaker: Piers
entries.text: Can you have a little sound bite about what has been the major successes in your view of the farm?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Okay. The major success in the first year was just the amount of food that we were able to grow on a really small space in a really contracted space season. The project was late getting started. You know, we were still waiting for bits of kit to arrive. The weather was awful, so we didn't really get cultivating until May, even sort of into June. So between then and the new year, we produced six and a half tons of food from about a third of an acre. So, you know, we were quite pleased with that. That was a major success. I think also we've been really successful at using the capital aspect of our grant funding to put in all the infrastructure that we need. So, you know, from a market gardener's point of view, you know, it's been great. It's all singing, all dancing. We've got all the polytunnels, we've got the irrigation, you know, we've got all the tools that we need. We've been able to buy all the seeds and seed trays and all that kind of stuff. So I think we've been really successful at using our resources quite carefully and to the best advantage. But I would say the main success, the thing that really gets me fired up is the fact that that food is going into schools, it's going into local care homes, we've been selling to local businesses. We're also providing into the third sector as well. So Fair Share Cymru had quite a lot of produce from us last summer. Local food banks, community kitchens and all of that has just been an absolutely brilliant networking experience. So we've really got to know a lot of people around the county who are involved in developing the food system in all these kind of discrete areas. And it's just been great to kind of link up all of these different cool things that are kind of going on around place and bring it all together under the kind of umbrella of the Local Food Partnership. So that's been really. That's been really successful and really exciting.

entries.speaker: Piers
entries.text: Amazing. Last question. What advice would you give to someone else trying to design kind of a farm that's more socially motivated and more sustainable?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: The main thing is really go out and speak to your community. If you want to develop something for the community on a farm like this, you can't do it top down. You can't come in and say we're going to design something like this and then bring the community in. You have to bring the community in right at the beginning and work with them, talk to them about what they want and what they need and then bring that into the design process. That doesn't mean that there shouldn't be oversight because there's going to be lots and lots of different ideas to kind of pull in together. So it does need an individual or a committee that are going to kind of bring all of those different elements together in the most successful way but there's nothing better than actually talking to the community about what they want and then beyond that really it's remembering that community isn't just about people is also about the natural environment as well. So if you want to have genuine community benefit then it has to have ecological benefit as well so you need to think about those things in the round. Really great.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, so I just started the recording. So just for, for the transcript, we're debriefing the whales case one which you might have to help me with the pronunciation.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Oh, I'm not gonna remember it now.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Serigar food.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Which, yeah, I just called it the Local Food Partnership in interviews after butchering the pronunciation on the first call. But you can, you can look up the exact pronunciat. Okay, good call.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, so, well, the first question is really just about your experience with contacting them and how did you hear about them, how was it getting in touch with them, Any kind of difficulties or any particular thing that's interesting to know.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah. So I was aware of local food partnerships as a concept, just based on previous academic research and also had a lot of connections to the Welsh food landscape, given my previous dissertation work on free school meals at the Welsh level. So I was kind of aware of some of the players in kind of the local food ecosystem. I selected Kamarntha Shear based on its development in a local food partnership. I think they had a lot more set up than some of the other ones in Wales. And based on that, I reached out to a few of my contacts in the Welsh foods space since they didn't have an email on the website for the two lead people. So I reached out to a couple people and I asked them to put me in contact with those, the two people that are running the Local Food Partnership. I think since I was connected to some other high level people in the Welsh food landscape, they were much more willing and quick to respond to me. I think I tried an alternate route first and I didn't hear from them. And then once they kind of knew I was connected with these other people, they were very accommodating and happy to meet with me. Not to say that they wouldn't have in the first place, but I think that just got my message through quicker. And then they were able to connect me to a lot of my supplementary interviews. So there's only one or two people that I kind of reached out to on LinkedIn who were also willing to help and did provide context. But for the most part the most useful interviews were just kind of snowball sampling after those first interviews.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, great, that's super useful to know. So can you give a quick overview of the project now? I mean, I know it's sort of like you did three interviews within this bigger project, so maybe you could give a little overview. The, the, the full, the full project and then what are the three that in particular here that we're going to talk about.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah. So for context, you know, a local Food partnership is kind of a loose association of different actors in the food system aiming to improve the local food system. So usually it's at the meso level. In this case it's part of a broader Welsh and UK network of local food partnerships. So they have some support from UK government and at the Welsh level, as well as private funders kind of supporting the establishment of these food partnerships and councils across Wales. So they're a little bit more structured than local food partnerships in other areas. And I was looking into, you know, to what extent do social contracts impact the success of the Local Food Partnership and its aims in Kamar Thanchier? So with that view, I hope to speak to people directly involved in that local food partnership as well as the Welsh level. I didn't speak to anyone at the UK level, but yeah, that was kind of the aim. I ended up speaking to people at the Local Food Partnership at their related farm. But that's not the main thing I was investigating was more the partnership as a whole that I wanted to assess. I also spoke to people at Kamarth and Shearer Council that have direct roles with it, the local Health Board, which also collaborates closely with the Local Food Partnership. I spoke with a representative of another local food partnership in Wales, just to get a different perspective. I also spoke to people at Sustainable Food Places, which is kind of the umbrella organization that helps provide administrative support and evaluation support for local food partnerships. But the main three that I videoed for the socioscope were Augusta Lewis, who is the main coordinator for the local Food Partnership. Piers, I'm forgetting his last name, but the head grower for the partnership, so their farm, which is one of their major success stories, spoke to him. And then I spoke to Alex Cooper, who was the main contact person at the County Carthashire County Council, kind of assisting with Local Food Partnership aims. And they were the three I spoke to that had like the most direct kind of experience with the Food Partnership and who were most responsible for its success. Like I said, I interviewed everyone else and they could be useful, but I thought kind of they, they got to the heart of like the main collaboration issues for the Food Partnership. Okay, great.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: And so what, what was the kind of motivation and the values underlying the, the launch of, of this initiative? Did you get a feel from speaking to them of like, how, how this was born and how it came to be? I mean, obviously it's, it's meso level, so there's a lot of. And it's it's counting and know, maybe state level. But did you get a feeling about kind of what was the motivation behind it and the values?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, like I said, it's operating at a lot of different levels. So there's you know, UK top down support for local food partnerships. So that's a whole different factor. But I think that's one of the reasons that this is so interesting. It operates like in a broader ecosystem of local food partnerships in Wales, whereas in England there's not as much of support for those across the counties or boroughs in England. So I think there's less kind of cohesion. But in terms of within Carmarthenshire I think just like there's a lot of community support for not duplicating effort and helping kind of the food actors within Wales to act together. So yeah, I think that was the main reason. Just wanting to improve the local food system and have more of a cohesive food strategy that aligned the different actors in that council.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, do you know when, when this kind of took off and, and.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, and blanking on the exact dates there in the transcript with Augusta. I think it's only been two or three years since it's formally gotten funding as in its current state. I think it's built off of prior work in Martha and Shear, but relatively new in the last four to five years. Okay, okay, cool.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: And so then in terms of their funding, it is quite heavily funded by the Welsh government. But did they also have other kind of investors or is it a mix of public and private? Like what did you learn about their kind of business model? I guess it's, it's a big network. So I realize that that's. It's different based on who you're talking to. But what did you understand about it?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, the primary funding comes I think even from UK government through Welsh government, through the council to the host organization, Social Farms and Gardens, which houses kind of the staff that run this partnership, which is interesting. And from what I can assess that's pretty much the status quo for Welsh Food Partnerships is an already established organization hosts the position focused on the Food Partnership. But yeah, it's mainly like short term grant funding like with like one to two year timeline. So pretty unstable in terms of being able to plan for the future. In this case study there is revenue I think generated from the farm. It's like kind of at a small commercial level. I don't know to what extent that supports operations, but beyond that I don't think there's a ton of other funding. I think there's the plan to do some community fundraising, but they're not doing that right now. Okay.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Just so I understand a little better. So is there kind of a core. I guess, core activities and core institutions that they're coordinating? Like Augusta is coordinating the. The program. Right. Is that, like, partly the farm, partly the kind of advocacy work they're doing and et cetera? I saw there's also, like, some. Some things I saw on their website about, like, restaurants that they're also sourcing their foods to and, like, other types of partnerships. I'm guessing that's separate, but, like, for this actual local Food partnership, are there some kind of core activities that they're working on, or is it.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, their website lists all of their top projects, because there. There's a lot. The main one in the past has been the development of the Food Strategy that the Council can adopt. I think they've already accomplished that, so it's more getting actors to align and implement the Food Strategy. Their main project is obviously the farm and at the farm doing education, both for farmers and community members. So Augusta kind of coordinates that. Also volunteer opportunities, both at the Farm and, you know, other food policy organizations in the region. Yeah, lots of kind of diverse education and volunteer opportunities. I think they're trying to coordinate and then just also getting different actors to align on initiatives. So they do a lot of collaboration with the Health Board, making sure Food Strategy complements health recommendations. They're doing some outreach to, I think, underserved communities helping deliver food and kind of set up a program related to that. Okay. Okay, great.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Can we go through a bit of, like, a stakeholder grid, just looking at who are the different actors and stakeholders that exist within this and. And who they're working with, and then talking a little bit about the relationships of, like, what they give, what they get. Is it a positive, negative, or neutral relationship? How important is it to them?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah. Do you want me to just go.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Through, or let's just go kind of, like, one by one? Yeah.

entries.speaker: Samara
