entries.text: So there's the Food Partnership itself, and then other important actors kind of at the county level include the council itself, which obviously provides funding as well as administrative support. Overall, I would say that was a positive relationship. I think, given that kind of the Food Partnership does some of the work that might be expected of the Council in terms of food Strategy. There is some, like, duplication of efforts, and I think, overall, that's a main thing that's difficult with the food partnerships. Like, is this the responsibility of the council, the Food Partnership, the Health Board. I think that was a major source of tension throughout this whole system. There's also the local Health board, which is kind of a local division of the National Welsh Health Board. They're a huge actor. They do a ton of collaboration with the Food Partnership. Same thing overall, super positive. Seems like everyone's aligned on improving food and nutrition in the region, but some duplication of effort. So I would say that was the only negative source attention between them. There's also the host organization, Social Farms and Gardens. Like I said, typically the Local Food Partnerships don't establish themselves as a business entity or a nonprofit. They kind of operate through an existing organization. So I think between the Local Food Partnership and the. The host organization, it's pretty positive. I spoke to people both on the host organization and the Local Food Partnership and they had a positive relationship. I think it was mainly Augusta doing most of the work and just the organization providing the hosting and admin support, like hr, et cetera. I think those are the main players. Beyond like schools and. And other support organization or other food organizations in the region. The schools get, you know, the benefit of education, the produce produced by the farm volunteer and education opportunities for the children and staff. The Local Food Partnership also provides a teacher training program where they teach teachers, school facilitators, the importance of nutrition, etc. I don't know exactly what that teaching entails, but it's free and provided to teachers free of charge, so lots of community benefits. I think it's a little difficult for the staff of the Local Food Partnership to facilitate everything given that they have such a small support team. So sometimes with scheduling difficulties with the schools or with managing kind of demand for their services and training opportunities, there's some difficulties. And then overall, I would say positive benefits with other Local Food Partnerships. I think the Local Food Partnership tries to amplify their impact. I would say the only thing is, like the Local Food Partnership is its own entity. So sometimes I think other food organizations can be kind of left out, maybe of advocacy or, you know, grant funding, because they're not officially part of the Local Food Partnership because it's its own entity. Beyond that, at the Welsh level, there's FoodSense Wales, which is kind of the national branch of Sustainable Food Places, which provides kind of administrative, like I said, administrative support, evaluation support, and just works as a general convener for the Local Food Partnerships at the Welsh and UK level. So overall I think they have a positive relationship with the Local Food Partnerships. I think kind of capacity within both Foodsense Wales and Sustainable Food Places makes it so they can only provide so much support for the different food partnerships. So there's a bit of tension around that. But yeah, overall I think there's like positive relationships between most of the organizations. Everyone seemed like they were pretty aligned on their objectives.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Okay, how big is the project? Like it seems like it's fairly big with a lot of different actors and like a lot of reach. Or would you say it's still like fairly small to like the county or.

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: I mean the Food Partnership itself is county based, but there's I think one to two full time staff, about three full time staff on the farm. I would say probably like 40 to 100 people like to some extent involved in its initiatives in some capacity. And then I think it's pretty well established as a source of community and farmer support. Yeah, it's hard to, hard to say because it's kind of like somewhat of an informal organization in a lot. Okay.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: No, but that's already useful what you just said. Super. So you already kind of went into some of the challenges or like that's great that you've been kind of flagging what the challenges are as you've been also talking about the, the stake closure, the actors. Are there any specific challenges they face that you haven't mentioned yet that you think are interesting to know about and then also like on the other side of it, what are the kind of enabling things that allow them to continue this work and, and grow the project?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, I think some of the big ones, like I mentioned there some duplication of effort, efforts like a, across different people, especially at the council level. And I do think the fact that there's not, you know, really a governance structure that focuses on food specifically within, I mean any government, but like within the council structure, like there's not really a food officer within the county council. So they have to create these local food partnerships. But then those people only have so much leverage over council proceedings. And there's also some duplication on, you know, there's the health board perspective on things and then there's the council perspective and the local Food Partnership perspective. So I think while the Local Food Partnership does enable like yeah, some, you know, there's now someone in the council, though not within the government prioritizing food kind of. I think their reach is limited based on the lock in of current governance practices. So that's a huge one. I think the other main thing from all the actors perspectives is short term funding cycles is it's really difficult to do long term planning and to really establish the food Partnership, especially since the funding is so small and a lot of the work is being done by a small team. Yeah, there's just like only so much that they can do in terms of long term planning and building up their support services. So those are I think the two, two main challenges. I think. Yeah. The third one I talked about with them was proving the usefulness of these kind of. Or the usefulness of the food partnership overall beyond kind of direct impact like food produced for this thing or number, number of educational workshops, you know, held is a little bit difficult because some of the impacts of this are more intangible long term benefits like development of a cohesive food culture or support for organic food over the long term. Like these are all very like hard to evaluate long term metrics. So trying to prove the support for this kind of funding and government structure is difficult. So I think those are the big three problems in terms of what is enabling all of this. I do think the nationwide strategy is a super effective thing. Like I've spoken to people in England who have local food partnerships and they don't have that same cohesive top down framework for. Or not even. Yeah, I would say framework. Like not each local food partnership is the same, but there's a lot of learning across the different food partnerships about how, how to establish themselves, how to deal with challenges. I think that really helps. And there's like, despite the funding being like short term there, there is kind of this nationwide pool of funding for local food partnerships specifically, which is great. I think the cohesiveness as Wales as a, you know, small developed nation also helps a lot. I think there's more of a sense of community there that's more of like a just appealing that, that I perceive for my interviews. But it felt like everyone was really aligned on these objectives and working together and they had a strong sense of the purpose of a local food partnership. Yeah, I think those are kind of the two, two main things that I think are we're holding things together. Okay.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Just curious about the people you interviewed themselves and like the role that they have in keeping this project going and like their passion for it. I know something we've seen in a lot of other cases is sometimes it like really comes down to the founders or the people who are at the heart of the project who are really keeping it moving. So I'm just curious, is that something that you felt with the people you've met or not necessarily?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, 100%. I would say everyone kind of working on this was probably dedicating outside of work time or, you know, passion for making this work and putting in a lot of effort in the short term to kind of hopefully set up the, you know, set up the project so that there was long term impact and stability. Even, you know, the head grower and the head coordinator married. So, you know, it was clearly like a family effort to make this whole project work and a lot of time was spent on it. So, yeah, I definitely think everyone was very passionate and had a personal stake in improving the local food system. Yeah. Okay, great.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Did you get a chance to hear about any kind of future plans or any developments that they're trying to work on?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Yeah, yeah. Like I said, they have so many different projects and objectives, it's hard to overview them all. I think continuing to advocate for and implement the food strategy that they develop was a huge priority. Continuing to work closely with the council and the health board to implement that and develop other offerings to improve the food system locally. With regards to the farm, they had plans in the next like five to 10 years to establish like a community area, the farm because due to some farming regulations, they can't have children and school groups on the current farm since it's producing commercially. So they want to develop more like a volunteer farming area as well as currently they're renting the farm that they're on and they hope to bid for its tenancy in the next two to three years. So that was a huge objective they were working towards. They plan to with some new grant funding that they received on board, a full time volunteer coordinator as well as some other staff people. So kind of building up the team is another objective in the next couple years so they can diversify what Augusta is working on as well as the support that they can provide different community members. Okay, great.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: Do you think that, you know, because part of the work for the Socioscope is like right now we were finishing up the first phase of different interviews and cases that we've been able to find. And then we would like to go back to some of them, if we can, in the next phase to kind of delve more into like, what is the network, how they work together, but also in terms of like what they're trying to develop, see like what that looks like in the coming, coming years. Do you think that this could be a case that would be interesting for us to go back to and interview again?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: Definitely, I think they'd be willing to speak again. They were very helpful. I think that they have some interesting new developments in the next couple years and the potential to see, like, will there be additional funding for these objectives at the Welsh level or not? And I think local food partnerships as a concept are very interesting because like I said, they are kind of like these informal arrangements a lot on social contracts and aligning different stakeholder objectives. So I think from like a socioscope perspective, it would be really interesting to see like, does this kind of more ground up, you know, still with still some top down support, but somewhat ground up approach to prioritizing food at the local level work, or do we need to kind of have more governance arrangements but where you would just prioritize more from the council directly? Okay, cool.

entries.speaker: Julia
entries.text: No, definitely. That sounds super interesting. Do you have any other insights you want to share about the case? Like, I've asked you like very specific questions, but anything else that's like particularly interesting that you learned about maybe like the network and the stakeholder kind of the social contracts you mentioned? Super interesting. Yeah. Anything else you want to add?

entries.speaker: Samara
entries.text: No, I think it'd be interesting to like speak to other local food partnerships too, long term, both in Wales and in other nations. I think kind of the insights there would be super interesting for like, what, what makes the local food partnership work? Well, are there kind of differences in the cultural approach to them across countries? But yeah, I think we covered most of everything. Okay, great, thanks.
